summary: The post examines comparative advantage theory—the economic argument that countries (or spouses) should specialize in what they're relatively better at—and systematically dismantles its real-world applications. O'Neil explains the mathematical validity of the concept while cataloging its failures: job displacement, diminishing returns, technological inequality, and most critically, its use as justification for exploitation. She then applies this critique to household labor division in marriage, arguing that 'scientific' allocation of chores based on comparative advantage inevitably disadvantages women who have been socialized to be more efficient at domestic work. The post concludes that marriages should optimize for minimizing resentment rather than maximizing economic efficiency.
key_points: Comparative advantage is mathematically valid but fails in practice due to job losses, diminishing returns, technological disparities, and monoculture risks
The theory is weaponized to justify exploitation—telling Bangladesh to accept poverty wages and women to accept doing 75% of housework as 'scientifically optimal'
Women's apparent 'advantage' at housework is a product of training and expectation, not innate ability, and grows larger with practice
Economic theories applied to marriage ignore that we don't actually measure time spent on tasks, and the measurement itself poisons relationships
Marriage should optimize for minimizing long-term resentment, not productivity—sometimes doing tasks you hate helps you understand your partner's experience
money_quotes: What is most troublesome about the modern worshipping of comparative advantage is that we end up using it as an excuse to exploit people.
Given existing conditions, the US should make computers, not work very hard, and be rich, Bangladesh should stitch T-shirts for Old Navy at 30 cents an hour, work really hard, and be poor.
In the sexist soup straight couples all swim in, 'don't keep score, everybody do what you're best at' seems to invariably end up at the equilibrium 'woman does 75% of the shitwork.'
What the comparative advantage crowd says is you are not even allowed to be mad about this, women, it is ratified by science.

One of the problems with applying an economic theory to a marriage is that we don't actually keep track of how much time it takes us to do various things, and even if we did do that we'd probably do it wrong.
My advice to married couples is to ignore scientific arguments, and instead think about a system that will minimize longterm resentment, which is poison in any marriage or relationship.

The essential ingredients in any marriage is a sense of teamwork, the dedication to alleviating the other person's suffering, and a promise to encouraging one another's fulfillment. And economics doesn't have much to say about those things.
key_insight: Economic theories like comparative advantage, however mathematically elegant, become tools of oppression when applied to human relationships—whether between nations or spouses—because they rationalize existing power imbalances as optimal outcomes.
